attributes.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a two month history of increasing difficulty in swallowing, to the extent that he can now only manage liquids. During this time he has lost 10 kg in weight. He has a 45 pack-year smoking history and consumes about four cans of beer a day. He is still active and enjoys lawn bowls. On examination he appears thin, but there are no specific abnormal findings. Endoscopy confirms a stenosing tumour in his mid oesophagus, which on biopsy proves to be a squamous cell carcinoma. CT staging shows the bulky tumour, with several enlarged mediastinal nodes. There are several metastatic deposits in segments II and VIII of the liver.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Nivolumab with a chemotherapy regimen of 5-fluorouracil and cisplatin
attributes.explanation:
This patient has evidence of tissue invasion, lymph node metastases and distant metastasis, indicating Stage 4 oesophageal carcinoma. There are several aspects to management that need to be considered. First, is the disease so widespread and advanced that a palliative approach must be considered? Second, his primary problem is dysphagia and how is this to be handled? Third, if the cancer is to be treated, what treatment will be most effective? In this case the disease cannot be cured, but the patient appears to be reasonably active and the aim of treatment should to improve his quality of life and hopefully improve his life expectancy.

Systemic chemotherapy and immunotherapy are the mainstays of treatment for symptomatic or imminently symptomatic stage 4 disease and should be considered for this patient.

Surgical resection is reserved as a treatment for resectable and potentially curable disease (locally invasive disease), which is not in keeping with this patient's presentation. He may need some sort of intervention, such as a mesh stent, to improve his swallowing.

A chemotherapy regimen of 5-fluorouracil and cisplatin as a regimen has not been shown to be as effective as immunotherapies.

Nivolumab with a chemotherapy regimen of 5-fluorouracil and cisplatin has been shown to be superior to chemotherapy alone with respect to overall survival. Nivolumab is an immune checkpoint inhibitor and monoclonal antibody. By itself, nivolumab is used to treat other Stage 4 cancers, such as metastatic melanoma.

Nivolumab plus ipilimumab has been shown to be superior to chemotherapy alone but still does not show the same increase in survival as seen with nivolumab plus chemotherapy regimen of 5-FU and cisplatin.

Radiation as primary therapy for metastatic oesophageal SCC has not been researched enough to be justified as a treatment.

Statistics of the overall survival between each regimen of chemotherapy and immunotherapy treatment (reference 2):

Nivolumab plus ipilimumab showed significantly longer overall survival compared to chemotherapy in the overall population. The median survival for nivolumab plus ipilimumab was 12.7 months (95% CI, 11.3 to 15.5) compared to 10.7 months (95% CI, 9.4 to 11.9) with chemotherapy alone.

Nivolumab plus chemotherapy, compared to chemotherapy alone, has a significantly longer overall survival in the overall population. The median survival for Nivolumab plus chemotherapy was 13.2 (95% CI, 11.1 to 15.7) months compared to 10.7 months (95% CI, 9.4 to 11.9) for chemotherapy alone.
learningPoints: Studies have shown that treatment of advanced oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma with nivolumab plus chemotherapy has significantly longer overall survival
Systemic chemotherapy and immunotherapy are the mainstay of treatment for stage 4 disease
references: Doki Y, Ajani JA, Kato K, Xu J, Wyrwicz L, Motoyama S, Ogata T, Kawakami H, Hsu CH, Adenis A, El Hajbi F. Nivolumab combination therapy in advanced esophageal squamous-cell carcinoma. New England Journal of Medicine. 2022 Feb 3;386(5):449-62.
Mackay S, Stefanou G. Management of oesophageal carcinoma. Australian family physician. 2006 Apr 1;35(4):202.
